metadata.summary: Design methodology for interfaces that combine voice input with visual display, pattern-matching voice commands to card/carousel output while maintaining touch fallbacks for every voice state.

content.title: Multimodal VUI Design
content.summary: Design methodology for interfaces that combine voice input with visual display — pattern-matching voice commands to on-screen card/carousel output while maintaining touch fallbacks for every voice state. Every dialogue turn must declare: voice prompt, visual state (max 5 items), valid touch actions, and silence fallback.

why.p: Voice-only interfaces cannot display structured data; screen-only interfaces add friction for hands-free or glanceable contexts. Multimodal design bridges them, but amplifies errors when done poorly — missing touch fallbacks strand users who switch modalities mid-task. The per-turn structure enforces completeness before prototyping.

list.item: Smart TV, smart display, kiosk, or automotive HMI combining voice with a screen
Voice assistants surfacing structured data (product cards, search results, maps)
Accessibility-first products where users alternate between touch and voice
Conversational commerce (search → carousel → voice-confirm purchase)
Audio-only environments (earbuds, phone IVR) — no screen; use pure VUI
Text-heavy B2B tools (dashboards, IDEs) — voice adds friction with no multimodal benefit
Products where latency >2s is unacceptable — ASR + LLM + TTS chain is inherently slow
Teams without dedicated voice UX expertise — multimodal amplifies design errors
Voice-initiated, screen-completed — user speaks a query; screen displays structured results (cards, carousels); voice summarizes aloud.
Screen-initiated, voice-completed — user taps a form field or list item; voice confirms or elaborates.
Voice + visual feedback — confirmations and progress states shown on screen while TTS reads them.
Voice navigation + visual content — user says "next" or "go back" while screen renders the content.

rule.statement: Every dialogue turn must declare four fields: voice_prompt (what the assistant says, max 15 words for smart display), visual_state (component type + content, max 5 items), user_actions (valid voice inputs AND touch actions), and fallback (what happens after 5 seconds of silence).
rule.rationale: Incomplete turns produce states reachable only by voice — users who switch to touch are stranded. The structure enforces completeness at design time.

rule.statement: Every voice state must have a touch equivalent. If a user cannot complete the task using touch alone, the design is not multimodal — it is voice-only with a broken screen.
rule.rationale: Users switch modalities mid-session constantly: privacy concerns, noisy environment, screen reader mode. Modal lock-in is a usability and accessibility failure.

rule.statement: Limit visual card content to 5 items maximum per screen state. For lists longer than 5, paginate and surface navigation affordances for both voice ("say 'next' for more") and touch.
rule.rationale: Smart display screens are small (7-10 inches). Card patterns that work on phones break on small displays. Cognitive overload from dense lists degrades task completion rates.

rule.statement: Define three input tiers: Primary (voice), Secondary (touch/tap), Tertiary (keyboard). No interaction should require voice as the only completion path.
rule.rationale: ASR accuracy varies by accent, noise, and microphone quality. A fallback-free voice-only path fails in the real environments where multimodal devices are deployed.

rule.statement: Display streaming visual feedback (waveform animation, typing indicator, partial content) during ASR processing and LLM response generation. Silence of more than 1 second during processing feels broken to users.
rule.rationale: LLM + TTS chain latency is 1-3 seconds. Without visual feedback, users repeat their command or abandon the interaction.

antipattern.description: Designing dialogue flows on a phone emulator before testing on target hardware
antipattern.reason: Emulators do not reproduce touch + voice timing, screen size constraints, or microphone quality. Test on actual device hardware before usability testing.

antipattern.description: Writing voice prompts without defining the persona voice first
antipattern.reason: Tone inconsistency across turns destroys user trust in the product's identity. Define persona before writing a single prompt.

list.item: gcloud (Google Cloud SDK) — access Dialogflow CX, Speech-to-Text, Text-to-Speech APIs; cloud.google.com/sdk
aws CLI — Amazon Alexa Skills Kit, Polly TTS, Transcribe; aws.amazon.com/cli
az CLI — Azure Cognitive Services (Speech SDK, LUIS); learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure
ffmpeg — audio processing and format conversion for TTS output validation; ffmpeg.org
Dialogflow CX (Google SaaS) — industry standard for multimodal flows; supports rich responses (cards, chips); REST API agent-friendly
Amazon Alexa Skills Kit — multimodal templates for Echo Show; APL for visual layer; REST/SDK
Voiceflow (SaaS) — visual dialogue builder with REST API export; good for rapid prototyping
ElevenLabs (SaaS) — high-quality TTS; agent can generate voice previews for design review; REST API
Rasa (OSS) — self-hosted NLU + dialogue management; Python API

rule.statement: A Claude subagent can generate a multimodal dialogue script (voice_prompt, visual_state, user_actions, fallback) and validate it programmatically. Visual implementation (actual screen layouts, component assembly) requires human designer review.
rule.rationale: Agents cannot validate touch target sizing, card visual hierarchy, or readability at target screen size without a rendering environment.

antipattern.description: Agent-generated dialogue defaults to formal register regardless of persona brief
antipattern.reason: Prompt explicitly for the target persona tone (e.g., "casual, helpful, under 12 words per turn"). Without explicit constraints, LLMs default to formal assistant register.

antipattern.description: Agent assumes instant LLM response with no latency design
antipattern.reason: LLM response latency in a live voice chain is user-visible (1-3s). Agents must design streaming visual feedback for every turn involving an LLM call.

antipattern.description: Agent generates a dialogue tree referencing visual card components without verifying they exist in the design system
antipattern.reason: Always cross-reference generated component types against the actual component library. Non-existent components produce broken screens.

antipattern.description: Agent assumes high ASR accuracy in dialogue design
antipattern.reason: ASR accuracy varies significantly by accent and environment. Designs must include disambiguation turns and robust "I didn't understand" fallbacks for all critical paths.

list.item: Design every voice state to have a touch equivalent — users switch modalities mid-session constantly
Keep voice prompts under 15 words for smart display; longer prompts lose users before visual renders
Use streaming visual feedback (e.g., waveform animation) during ASR processing — silence feels broken
Test on actual device hardware before usability testing — emulators do not reproduce touch + voice timing accurately
Define a clear persona voice before writing any prompts — tone inconsistency across turns destroys trust
Version control dialogue scripts separately from visual assets; they have different change cadences
Google Conversation Design documentation — developers.google.com/assistant/conversation-design
Amazon Alexa Multimodal Design Guidelines — developer.amazon.com/en-US/alexa/alexa-skills-kit/design
Designing Voice User Interfaces — Cathy Pearl, O'Reilly 2017
Voiceflow Multimodal Design Guide — voiceflow.com/docs
Nielsen Norman Group — Voice Interfaces research — nngroup.com

template.purpose: JSON schema for a single multimodal dialogue turn
Script to validate a dialogue tree for structural completeness and missing touch fallbacks
Agent prompt: generate multimodal dialogue script for a given task and device
